references.title: When the Past != The Future: Assessing the Impact of Dataset Drift on the Fairness of Learning Analytics Models

extracted.Features: [Gender, Age, Citizenship Status, Home Language, Last LMS login, Medium of Contact, Study Period, Course Accesses, Resourcee Acesses, Assignment Accesses, Quiz Accesses, Forum Accesses, Folder Accesses, Page Accesses, URL accesses]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The authors decide to do feature selection using the top-5 features for each year unsing the RF feature importances.

To do this, the authors rank the feature importances in each year and select the top-5 moist important ones.

Authors also subset their analysis across distinct demographiic segments:

- English vs Non-English speakers
- Citizens vs Non-Citizens
extracted.Comments:
Most datassets in the real world are “not stationary”.  A fair algorithm trained at time t has no assurance of remaining fair at time t+1…

The authors m,ake an assessment of dataset drift in Learning Analytics student success models in 2 perspectives… how performance is affected from one academic year to next (it is…) and also how this is affected in terms of model fairness when comparing generral student populations (citizens orr english speakers vs other demographics).
extracted.Limitations:
- Small number of courses in STEM.

- Vulnerable to changes in instructional conditions from year to year.

- Only tested on Randoom Forest

- No early prediction

annotations.text: The authors analyse the drift in features (covariate drift) and drift of the target. The results show that the features are the most responsible for unfairness of the models in the dynamic context. Moreover, there is also a proportional relationship between drift and model unfairness. Finally, there is no guarantee that a fair model will remain fair → requiring models to be robust before deployment.
